figures.caption: Figure 2: Empirical total gradient variance Tr(Cov[∇θℓMF]) on six two-dimensional toy datasets with β ∈ {0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1}. The monotonic decrease of variances with respect to β on almost every dataset aligns with the prediction in Theorem 2.
Figure 7: 100 class-conditional samples from the β=1 corner checkpoint at step 300k (FID 23.36). Same noise seed and class labels as figs. 5 and 6.

Figure 3: Empirical sample-quality measured by sliced Wasserstein-1 distance SW1(β) on six 2- D toy datasets with β ∈ {0.0, 0.1, . . . , 1.0}. Bars are grouped by dataset and colored by β with standard error bar. The red star marks the empirical optimal coefficient β∗. Five of six datasets settle at β∗∈{0.8, 1.0} near the deterministic-tangent corner; two spirals alone settles at β∗=0.
Figure 5: 100 class-conditional samples from the β = 0 baseline checkpoint at step 300k (FID 11.37). Same noise seed and class labels as figs. 6 and 7.

Figure 4: Experiment results training DiT-B/4 on ImageNet-256. Left: Per-step loss variance under the stochastic (β = 0) versus deterministic (β = 1) tangent, measured on the baseline (β = 0) checkpoint with the same data and noise. The deterministic-tangent variance is roughly t-flat while the stochastic-tangent variance grows by two orders of magnitude with t, confirming the Jacobifactor amplification of Theorem 2. Right: FID at converged with respect to different β. We report empirical ∆FID(β) = FID(β)−FID(β = 0) at the matched training step 295k with different β. The dashed gray curve is the quadratic cβ2. The FID at converge with β =1 sits a factor of ∼ 2.6 above the quadratic curve, indicating a super-linear relationship between the FID and the bias. The optimal coefficient calculated in matrix form is β∗ matrix≈0.94.
Figure 1: Spatial distribution of √ Tr(Σv′ |xt) = √ Ex0|xt ∥v′∥2 at three timesteps on a twodimensional Gaussian mixture. Conditional variances concentrate in mode-mixing regions.
Figure 6: 100 class-conditional samples from the β=0.5 checkpoint at step 300k (FID 12.51). Same noise seed and class labels as figs. 5 and 7.

Table 4: Sliced Wasserstein distance (lower is better) on the 2-D toy benchmark, averaged over three seeds {42, 0, 1} at 200k steps. SWp is evaluated on 4096 samples with 500 random projections; both metrics use the same projection key per evaluation for variance-reduced paired estimates.

Table 5: DGMM β-sweep: full grid of mean±SEM SW1 at convergence (200k steps, three seeds {42, 0, 1}). Bolded cell per column marks the empirical β∗=argminβ SW1(β) at that dimension. The summary view is in Table 2.
